lessons_learned: The lack of a clear objective definition may result in multiple and complex objectives which creates unnecessary difficulties during implementation and the subsequent evaluation of results.

The proper inclusion of private pilot and/or demonstration subprojects requires an adequate definition of procedures, ideally in the form of a detailed Subproject Manual produced during project preparation, and not during the initial phase of implementation, to maximize the operation period of financed technologies.

The fulfillment of the project objectives should not be conditioned to the co-financers' decisions and actions, particularly in those cases where the execution of the investment committed does not include a significant contribution of project resources.

The requirement of the 10:1 co-financing ratio is significantly high, because of this, it must be duly interpreted and evaluated by the preparation team, and incorporated into the design differentiating the co-financers' commitment from the potential contribution of the planned investments to the outcome indicators.

Biovalor demonstrated that effective inter-ministerial coordination becomes crucial not only for an adequate governance, but also for analyzing cross-cutting issues and generating stronger solutions agreed upon by all parties. Also, in projects that combine regulatory and technology adoption goals by the productive sector, the joint development of operational coordination instruments with the academia, research activities, and the private sector is essential to ensure the viability of the actions implemented.

In order to maintain actions relevant and consistent, some flexibility in implementation becomes critical to allow for eventual adjustments to the original design parameters. To achieve this, a technically qualified and committed PMU is required, together with an active and representative governance, and the active involvement of UNIDO.

UNIDO's contribution to the success of the project would have been more effective, had the mid-term review been more comprehensive and focused on adjustments to overcome difficulties and/or had introduced new implementation activities during the remaining execution period.
The beneficiaries' motivation and profile represent a key factor and they should be included in the subproject selection matrix.

Both during project design and implementation, it is essential to define whether the subprojects to be supported pursue a pilot objective (experimentation of new technologies) or a demonstration objective (promoting the adoption of proven technologies).

The effective contribution of pilot projects to the project objectives requires a realistic execution schedule, with enough time for the implementation and demonstration of the proven technology, and for the technical and economic evaluation of its results. In practical terms and according to the experience gained, subproject selection, design and installation should not extend beyond the first 30 months of implementation.

It is reasonable for some pilot subprojects not to produce satisfactory results (e.g., the Tupamaé subproject, supported by Biovalor), but demonstrating that an application or technology is not viable can also be considered a lesson learnt and a positive outcome for the project.
recommendations: The Uruguayan government should continue promoting instruments to encourage the replication of Biovalor's successful experiences, especially the generation and use of biogas, nutrient circularity on dairy farms, the use of waste in meat processing plants and the installation of micro biodigesters in public institutions in urban centers.

It is necessary to define and develop the potential to generate even greater environmental benefits through the promotion of waste processing technologies in other important production chains, such as industrial dairy processing plants, feed lots, and poultry and pig farming.

The MIEM, MGAP and DINAMA should continue their joint efforts to eliminate the barriers to competitiveness and expansion of some of the technologies tested, such as the lack of incentives for manufacturers and suppliers for equipment and inputs linked to project technologies, and the tax burden on compost marketing.

The environmental authority, together with the Ministry of Economy and Finance, should seek further dissemination of COMAP as an incentive instrument for private investment aimed at waste processing and decarbonization of production activities.

An inter-institutional effort is required to develop a technology and tax framework that promotes the viability and competitiveness of bio-products, including the development of appropriate technologies and the expansion of tax benefits for SMEs and small farmers contributing to IMESI.

ANDE, in coordination with Biovalor's executing bodies, should make the necessary efforts to ensure the continuity of the Circular Opportunities program and to obtain a budget allocation similar to that of 2019, in line with the demands of the private sector.
With regard to gender, these aspects should be more explicitly mentioned in future project indicators.

Because it was not possible to complete its installation before the end of Biovalor, the pilot project of Rincón Blanco biodigester should receive active monitoring by DINAMA to ensure its proper completion within the agreed deadline (30 June, 2021).

It would be important to incorporate the lessons learnt from the project and to allocate resources for specific actions to consolidate Biovalor's results in the design of the new GEF-financed project managed by UNIDO in Uruguay.

UNIDO should assess the possibility of: (i) reviewing the methodology for recruiting and including co-financers into the project's costs and results matrix; (ii) providing additional flexibility for procurement procedures for private pilot/demonstration projects; (iii) expanding the scope of the mid-term review to identify possible restructuring needs of activities, budget allocation and indicators; and (iv) reviewing project management criteria and responsibilities to achieve greater decentralization of project management and monitoring functions.
executive_summary: This Independent Terminal Evaluation assessed the Biovalor project — 'Towards a Green Economy in Uruguay: Stimulating Sustainable Practices and Low-Emission Technologies in Prioritized Sectors' — implemented by UNIDO with GEF funding. The project aimed to reduce GHG emissions and improve waste management in Uruguay by fostering a transition towards a circular, low-emission economy through four core technologies: composting, anaerobic digestion, nutrient circularity, and biofuels. Delivered across five components, the project strengthened the environmental policy and regulatory framework, fostered private sector investment in relevant technologies, built capacities across academia, the public, and private sectors, and implemented pilot and demonstration subprojects on existing commercial operations with satisfactory results. A key strength was the effective inter-institutional coordination achieved among ministries, the Project Management Unit's efficient technical management, and active UNIDO local representation support. The project was rated Satisfactory overall, exceeding targets in several components and generating multiple positive results with good prospects for future expansion. Challenges included low execution rates in early years, limited scope in certain high-priority waste-generating sectors, and some subprojects facing delays. Sustainability prospects were assessed as moderately likely, contingent on continued government commitment to the regulatory and financial instruments developed. The evaluation generated eleven lessons applicable to future projects and made ten recommendations to the Uruguayan government and UNIDO covering technology replication, regulatory development, institutional coordination, and improvements to project design methodology.
thematic_justification: The Biovalor project explicitly targeted waste valorization technologies — composting, anaerobic digestion, nutrient circularity, and biofuels — to reduce GHG emissions and improve waste management in Uruguay's agricultural and food processing sectors, placing it squarely within Circular Economy / Waste Management with a strong Climate Action dimension.

sdg_mapping.12: The project's core focus on promoting waste processing and valorization technologies — converting agricultural and food-processing waste into compost, biogas, and biofuels — directly contributes to SDG 12 on responsible consumption and production.
sdg_mapping.13: By reducing GHG emissions through the adoption of low-carbon waste management and production technologies, the project contributes to SDG 13 on urgent climate action.
sdg_mapping.15: The project's efforts to reduce effluent discharge into Uruguay's waterways and address eutrophication pollution contributed to SDG 15 on life on land and the protection of terrestrial and freshwater ecosystems.
sdg_mapping.2: By promoting nutrient circularity, biodigestion, and sustainable waste management practices in the agriculture and agro-industry sectors, the project supports SDG 2 on sustainable food systems and responsible agricultural production.

context.title: Independent Terminal Evaluation: Towards a Green Economy in Uruguay: Stimulating Sustainable Practices and Low-Emission Technologies in Prioritized Sectors
